toolUseResult.originalFile:
==== Decision

IAM propagation delays for Cloud Build v2 connection creation are handled by
connection-creation retry with exponential backoff, not by an impersonation
readiness gate or fixed sleep delays.

* **SA binding propagation**: `setIamPolicy` and `getIamPolicy` calls that fail with HTTP 400 "does not exist"
  (newly-created service account not yet visible to the IAM policy service) are retried with exponential backoff.
* **Secret access readiness**: The CB v2 connection creation LRO itself serves as the propagation test.
  If the Cloud Build Service Agent cannot yet access the secrets (IAM grant not propagated), the LRO fails.
  The caller retries with exponential backoff, deleting the zombie connection resource between attempts.
* **Secret resource propagation**: `getIamPolicy` on a newly created secret may return HTTP 404 while
  the Secret Manager resource propagates across Google's control plane.  The API documents that
  `getIamPolicy` returns an empty policy (HTTP 200) when no policy has been set — so 404 indicates
  the resource itself is not yet visible, not that the policy is absent.  After creating secrets,
  `rbgu_poll_until_ok` polls each secret's `getIamPolicy` endpoint until 200 before proceeding to
  grant IAM bindings.

==== Rationale

The connection creation LRO tests the actual operation, not a proxy.
When the Cloud Build Service Agent accesses resources requiring recently-granted permissions,
a successful LRO proves end-to-end readiness: the IAM grant has propagated,
the service agent identity is active, and the resource content is valid.

Fixed `sleep` delays ("sleep and pray") are unreliable:

* Google's IAM propagation documentation states changes "typically take less than 2 minutes"
  but provides no guaranteed upper bound.
* A 15-second sleep is too short for worst-case propagation and wastefully long for the common case.
* Retry-on-error tests the actual operation: if the grant has propagated, the retry succeeds immediately;
  if not, the retry continues until the deadline.

The `testIamPermissions` API cannot serve as a readiness gate because it only tests the caller's own
permissions on a resource -- it cannot test whether a third-party service account (like the CB service agent)
has been granted access to a secret.

==== Superseded: Impersonation Readiness Gate

The prior approach used an impersonation readiness gate: mint a short-lived token as the CB service agent
via `generateAccessToken`, then call `accessSecretVersion` with that impersonated token, retrying on 403
until the grant propagates.  This approach was abandoned due to three problems discovered 2026-03-03:

. **roles/owner does NOT grant iam.serviceAccounts.getAccessToken** --
  The explicit `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` grant (previously deleted as unnecessary per
  an earlier RBSCIP revision) was in fact required for impersonation.  Without it,
  `generateAccessToken` returns persistent 403.  roles/owner is a broad role but does not include
  the `iam.serviceAccounts.getAccessToken` permission needed for impersonation.

. **Wrong SA identity** -- The code impersonated the default build SA
  (`{PN}@cloudbuild.gserviceaccount.com`) but CB v2 connections use the Cloud Build Service Agent
  (`service-{PN}@gcp-sa-cloudbuild.iam.gserviceaccount.com`).  Even if impersonation worked,
  it would have tested the wrong principal.

. **No non-impersonation alternative exists** -- `testIamPermissions` and IAM Policy Analyzer
  reflect policy intent (what the policy document says), not enforcement state (whether the grant
  has actually propagated).  Only actual access as the principal proves propagation.

Pattern B (connection-creation retry) eliminates `generateAccessToken`, `accessSecretVersion`,
and any need for `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` grants for the readiness gate.

==== Evidence

[cols="1,3",options="header"]
|===
|Claim
|Source

|IAM changes propagate eventually, typically < 2 minutes
|https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/access-change-propagation[Access change propagation] -- "After you grant access, the access change takes effect after a period of time known as propagation delay"

|Newly created SA may not be immediately usable
|https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-create[Creating service accounts] -- "After you create a service account, you might need to wait 60 seconds or more before you use the service account"

|testIamPermissions tests caller permissions only
|https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/testing-permissions[Testing permissions] -- tests whether the caller has the specified permissions on a resource

|CB v2 connection creation uses the Cloud Build Service Agent to read secrets
|https://cloud.google.com/build/docs/automating-builds/github/connect-repo-github[Connect a GitHub host] -- the Cloud Build Service Agent must have `roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor` on the secrets used by the connection
|===

==== What Dies

* The impersonation readiness gate (`generateAccessToken` + `accessSecretVersion` as a pre-check)
* Any `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` grant solely for the readiness gate
  (build-time Mason impersonation grants are unrelated and preserved)
* Fixed `sleep 15` delays before CB v2 connection creation and after Governor SA creation
* Standalone `rbgu_poll_until_ok` as a correctness gate for IAM propagation
  (the SA GET poll remains as a fast-path existence check, not a grant propagation gate)
* Any assumption that SA existence implies SA usability in IAM policy operations

==== What Survives

* SA GET poll via `rbgu_poll_until_ok` -- retained as a fast-path check that the SA resource itself
  is readable, which typically resolves well before IAM policy propagation completes
* The existing 429/5xx transient retry loop in {rbtoe_iam_grant_project} step 3 --
  this handles a different failure class (rate limiting, server errors) and is preserved inside
  the new outer propagation retry
* All IAM grant functions ({rbtoe_iam_grant_project}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_repo},
  {rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket}) -- enhanced with propagation retry,
  not replaced
* Cloud Build service agent `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` grant on Mason SA --
  this is for build-time impersonation (Cloud Build executing as Mason), unrelated to the readiness gate

==== Backoff Profile

All propagation retries use the same profile:

* Initial delay: 3 seconds
* Multiplier: 2x
* Cap: 20 seconds
* Deadline: 420 seconds (7 minutes)

This profile accommodates Google's documented "typically < 2 minutes" with margin for worst-case
propagation while avoiding indefinite waits.

==== References

* Research conducted: 2026-03-03 (API documentation review, live 403 diagnosis)
* Google IAM documentation consistently describes eventual consistency without hard SLA bounds
* Pattern B (connection-creation-as-probe) replaces the original `generateAccessToken` / `accessSecretVersion` gate

-  (newly-created service account not yet visible to the IAM policy service) are retried with exponential backoff.

input.new_string:
==== Backoff Profile

All IAM-grant propagation retries — every site in `rbgi_IAM.sh` plus the
inline GAR retry in `rbgg_invest_director` — use the same single profile
locked in `rbgc_Constants.sh`:

* Initial delay: `RBGC_PROPAGATION_INITIAL_DELAY_SEC` (3 seconds)
* Multiplier: 2x (per-site arithmetic, not a kindle constant)
* Cap: `RBGC_PROPAGATION_MAX_DELAY_SEC` (20 seconds)
* Deadline: `RBGC_PROPAGATION_DEADLINE_SEC` (420 seconds; 7 minutes)

This profile accommodates Google's documented "typically < 2 minutes" with margin for worst-case
propagation while avoiding indefinite waits. It is the single budget shared
across all three propagation classes (A: forward member-visibility, B:
backward member-visibility, C: caller-recently-empowered); operator value
changes propagate to every grant site by editing the constants once. The
JWT-bearer consumer-side retry uses its own budget
(`RBGC_SA_KEY_CONSUMER_RETRY_*`) — different cost-of-waiting profile.

==== Backoff Profile

All IAM-grant propagation retries — every site in `rbgi_IAM.sh` plus the
inline GAR retry in `rbgg_invest_director` — use the same single profile
locked in `rbgc_Constants.sh`:

* Initial delay: `RBGC_PROPAGATION_INITIAL_DELAY_SEC` (3 seconds)
* Multiplier: 2x (per-site arithmetic, not a kindle constant)
* Cap: `RBGC_PROPAGATION_MAX_DELAY_SEC` (20 seconds)
* Deadline: `RBGC_PROPAGATION_DEADLINE_SEC` (420 seconds; 7 minutes)

This profile accommodates Google's documented "typically < 2 minutes" with margin for worst-case
propagation while avoiding indefinite waits. It is the single budget shared
across all three propagation classes (A: forward member-visibility, B:
backward member-visibility, C: caller-recently-empowered); operator value
changes propagate to every grant site by editing the constants once. The
JWT-bearer consumer-side retry uses its own budget
(`RBGC_SA_KEY_CONSUMER_RETRY_*`) — different cost-of-waiting profile.
toolUseResult.originalFile:
==== Decision

IAM propagation delays for Cloud Build v2 connection creation are handled by
connection-creation retry with exponential backoff, not by an impersonation
readiness gate or fixed sleep delays.

Three propagation classes are recognized by the IAM grant harness in
`zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate`, all sharing the same exponential-backoff
budget defined by the `RBGC_PROPAGATION_*` constants:

* **Class A — forward member-visibility**: `setIamPolicy` and `getIamPolicy`
  calls fail with HTTP 400 "does not exist" when a newly-created service
  account is not yet visible to the IAM policy service as a valid member.
* **Class B — backward member-visibility**: `setIamPolicy` and `getIamPolicy`
  calls fail with HTTP 400 "is not deleted" when a recently-deleted service
  account is still referenced in existing policy bindings.
* **Class C — caller-recently-empowered**: `getIamPolicy` / `setIamPolicy`
  on a resource-scope target (AR repo, SA, bucket, secret) returns HTTP 403
  because the caller's recently-granted role (e.g. fresh `roles/owner` on a
  newly-levied depot) has not yet reached the resource-scope IAM cache,
  even though it has reached project- and SA-scope caches.

Class C is time-bounded only — no body fingerprinting. The bound itself is
the discriminator: real propagation succeeds within budget, real denial
waits the budget and fails cleanly. The same model is already proven in
{rbtgo_access_jwt_probe}. Resource-scope grant sites declare 403 tolerance
with an empty body-glob; project-scope sites do not.

* **Secret access readiness**: The CB v2 connection creation LRO itself serves as the propagation test.
  If the Cloud Build Service Agent cannot yet access the secrets (IAM grant not propagated), the LRO fails.
  The caller retries with exponential backoff, deleting the zombie connection resource between attempts.
* **Secret resource propagation**: `getIamPolicy` on a newly created secret may return HTTP 404 while
  the Secret Manager resource propagates across Google's control plane.  The API documents that
  `getIamPolicy` returns an empty policy (HTTP 200) when no policy has been set — so 404 indicates
  the resource itself is not yet visible, not that the policy is absent.  After creating secrets,
  `rbgu_poll_until_ok` polls each secret's `getIamPolicy` endpoint until 200 before proceeding to
  grant IAM bindings.

==== Rationale

The connection creation LRO tests the actual operation, not a proxy.
When the Cloud Build Service Agent accesses resources requiring recently-granted permissions,
a successful LRO proves end-to-end readiness: the IAM grant has propagated,
the service agent identity is active, and the resource content is valid.

Fixed `sleep` delays ("sleep and pray") are unreliable:

* Google's IAM propagation documentation states changes "typically take less than 2 minutes"
  but provides no guaranteed upper bound.
* A 15-second sleep is too short for worst-case propagation and wastefully long for the common case.
* Retry-on-error tests the actual operation: if the grant has propagated, the retry succeeds immediately;
  if not, the retry continues until the deadline.

The `testIamPermissions` API cannot serve as a readiness gate because it only tests the caller's own
permissions on a resource -- it cannot test whether a third-party service account (like the CB service agent)
has been granted access to a secret.

==== Superseded: Impersonation Readiness Gate

The prior approach used an impersonation readiness gate: mint a short-lived token as the CB service agent
via `generateAccessToken`, then call `accessSecretVersion` with that impersonated token, retrying on 403
until the grant propagates.  This approach was abandoned due to three problems discovered 2026-03-03:

. **roles/owner does NOT grant iam.serviceAccounts.getAccessToken** --
  The explicit `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` grant (previously deleted as unnecessary per
  an earlier RBSCIP revision) was in fact required for impersonation.  Without it,
  `generateAccessToken` returns persistent 403.  roles/owner is a broad role but does not include
  the `iam.serviceAccounts.getAccessToken` permission needed for impersonation.

. **Wrong SA identity** -- The code impersonated the default build SA
  (`{PN}@cloudbuild.gserviceaccount.com`) but CB v2 connections use the Cloud Build Service Agent
  (`service-{PN}@gcp-sa-cloudbuild.iam.gserviceaccount.com`).  Even if impersonation worked,
  it would have tested the wrong principal.

. **No non-impersonation alternative exists** -- `testIamPermissions` and IAM Policy Analyzer
  reflect policy intent (what the policy document says), not enforcement state (whether the grant
  has actually propagated).  Only actual access as the principal proves propagation.

Pattern B (connection-creation retry) eliminates `generateAccessToken`, `accessSecretVersion`,
and any need for `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` grants for the readiness gate.

==== Evidence

[cols="1,3",options="header"]
|===
|Claim
|Source

|IAM changes propagate eventually, typically < 2 minutes
|https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/access-change-propagation[Access change propagation] -- "After you grant access, the access change takes effect after a period of time known as propagation delay"

|Newly created SA may not be immediately usable
|https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-create[Creating service accounts] -- "After you create a service account, you might need to wait 60 seconds or more before you use the service account"

|testIamPermissions tests caller permissions only
|https://docs.google.com/iam/docs/testing-permissions[Testing permissions] -- tests whether the caller has the specified permissions on a resource

|CB v2 connection creation uses the Cloud Build Service Agent to read secrets
|https://cloud.google.com/build/docs/automating-builds/github/connect-repo-github[Connect a GitHub host] -- the Cloud Build Service Agent must have `roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor` on the secrets used by the connection
|===

==== What Dies

* The impersonation readiness gate (`generateAccessToken` + `accessSecretVersion` as a pre-check)
* Any `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` grant solely for the readiness gate
  (build-time Mason impersonation grants are unrelated and preserved)
* Fixed `sleep 15` delays before CB v2 connection creation and after Governor SA creation
* Standalone `rbgu_poll_until_ok` as a correctness gate for IAM propagation
  (the SA GET poll remains as a fast-path existence check, not a grant propagation gate)
* Any assumption that SA existence implies SA usability in IAM policy operations

==== What Survives

* SA GET poll via `rbgu_poll_until_ok` -- retained as a fast-path check that the SA resource itself
  is readable, which typically resolves well before IAM policy propagation completes
* The existing 429/5xx transient retry loop in {rbtoe_iam_grant_project} step 3 --
  this handles a different failure class (rate limiting, server errors) and is preserved inside
  the new outer propagation retry
* All IAM grant functions ({rbtoe_iam_grant_project}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_repo},
  {rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket}) -- enhanced with propagation retry,
  not replaced
* Cloud Build service agent `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` grant on Mason SA --
  this is for build-time impersonation (Cloud Build executing as Mason), unrelated to the readiness gate

==== Backoff Profile

All propagation retries use the same profile:

* Initial delay: 3 seconds
* Multiplier: 2x
* Cap: 20 seconds
* Deadline: 420 seconds (7 minutes)

This profile accommodates Google's documented "typically < 2 minutes" with margin for worst-case
propagation while avoiding indefinite waits.

==== References

* Research conducted: 2026-03-03 (API documentation review, live 403 diagnosis)
* Google IAM documentation consistently describes eventual consistency without hard SLA bounds
* Pattern B (connection-creation-as-probe) replaces the original `generateAccessToken` / `accessSecretVersion` gate

file.content:
===== Propagation Error Strings

The HTTP 400 errors containing `"does not exist"` and `"is not deleted"` that
the grant functions retry on (per <<rbscip_trade_study>> backoff profile) are
**empirically stable but not contractually guaranteed**.
Google does not document error message text as stable API surface.
The HTTP status code (400) is the stable signal; the message content is
diagnostic, not machine-parseable contract.

The two patterns represent different propagation directions:

* `"does not exist"` — forward propagation: a newly-created {giam_service_account}
  is not yet visible to the IAM policy service as a valid member.  The SA
  resource has been created (GET returns 200) but the policy service has not yet
  learned about it.

* `"is not deleted"` — backward propagation: a recently-deleted
  {giam_service_account} is still referenced in existing policy bindings.
  The SA resource has been deleted but the policy service still considers it
  a valid (or at least referenced) member.

Multiple independent ecosystem sources (Terraform provider, Red Hat OpenShift
installer, Pulumi) have depended on these strings for years without breakage.
The Terraform Google provider explicitly treats HTTP 400 as retryable in IAM
contexts (issue #8280), which constitutes strong ecosystem evidence that this
pattern is reliable enough for major infrastructure tools.
The practical risk of string instability is low but nonzero.

**SA email vs numeric ID propagation.**
Google's documentation recommends using a service account's numeric ID rather
than its email address to reference it immediately after creation: "To identify
a service account just after it is created, use its numeric ID rather than its
email address."  Recipe Bottle uses email addresses throughout, which is why
propagation retry is necessary.  Switching to numeric ID could reduce (but
likely not eliminate) propagation delays.

**SA keys.create write-path propagation.**
Observed 2026-03-12 during fresh depot onboarding: `serviceAccounts.keys.create`
POST returned HTTP 404 "Service account ... does not exist" immediately after
both `serviceAccounts.get` (GET by email) and `serviceAccounts.keys.list` (GET)
succeeded on the same SA email path.  This demonstrates a third propagation
class: SA read operations (GET by email, keys.list) propagate independently
from SA write operations (keys.create) on the same endpoint family.
The 404 response confirms the POST was rejected without side effects — no key
was created — making retry safe.  `gcloud iam service-accounts keys list`
confirmed zero USER_MANAGED keys after the 404 failure.
The race is intermittent: a subsequent director creation (dir1011) with the same
code path succeeded on first attempt.
Mitigation: retry keys.create POST on 404 up to `RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_MAX`
times with `RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_DELAY_SEC` delay between attempts.

===== Secret Manager Regional Endpoints

Secret Manager supports both global and regional secret endpoints:

* Global: `projects/*/secrets/*`
* Regional: `projects/*/locations/*/secrets/*`

The service proto defines `additional_bindings` for regional secrets at both
`getIamPolicy` and `setIamPolicy`.  The current {rbtoe_iam_grant_secret} grants use global endpoints.  If Recipe Bottle ever uses regional

===== Propagation Error Catalog

Three propagation classes feed into `zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate`'s
per-site tolerance list (see <<rbscip_trade_study>> for the shared backoff
profile):

[cols="1,1,2,2,2",options="header"]
|===
|Class
|HTTP code
|Body glob
|Scope applicability
|Discriminator

|A — forward member-visibility
|400
|`*does not exist*`
|All grant sites (project + resource scope)
|Body match; message content stable across ecosystem

|B — backward member-visibility
|400
|`*is not deleted*`
|All grant sites (project + resource scope)
|Body match; message content stable across ecosystem

|C — caller-recently-empowered
|403
|`""` (empty — match any body)
|Resource-scope sites only ({rbtoe_iam_grant_repo}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_secret}, plus the inline GAR retry in {rbtgo_director_invest})
|Time-bound only; no body fingerprinting
|===

The HTTP 400 errors containing `"does not exist"` and `"is not deleted"` that
the grant functions retry on are **empirically stable but not contractually
guaranteed**.  Google does not document error message text as stable API
surface.  The HTTP status code (400) is the stable signal; the message
content is diagnostic, not machine-parseable contract.

The two 400 patterns represent different propagation directions:

* `"does not exist"` — forward propagation: a newly-created {giam_service_account}
  is not yet visible to the IAM policy service as a valid member.  The SA
  resource has been created (GET returns 200) but the policy service has not yet
  learned about it.

* `"is not deleted"` — backward propagation: a recently-deleted
  {giam_service_account} is still referenced in existing policy bindings.
  The SA resource has been deleted but the policy service still considers it
  a valid (or at least referenced) member.

Multiple independent ecosystem sources (Terraform provider, Red Hat OpenShift
installer, Pulumi) have depended on these strings for years without breakage.
The Terraform Google provider explicitly treats HTTP 400 as retryable in IAM
contexts (issue #8280), which constitutes strong ecosystem evidence that this
pattern is reliable enough for major infrastructure tools.
The practical risk of string instability is low but nonzero.

The HTTP 403 class C pattern represents **caller-empowerment propagation**
into resource-scope IAM caches: the caller (governor) has been freshly
granted `roles/owner` on the depot project, but the artifact-registry
resource-scope IAM cache has not yet learned about the grant, even though
project-scope and SA-scope caches already have (the asymmetry observed
2026-05-13 against `artifactregistry.repositories.getIamPolicy` on a
fresh-depot AR repo, with `roles/owner` granted ~30 seconds prior).
Cumulative additional propagation observed: ~30 seconds.

Class C uses **time-bound discrimination** rather than body matching: real
propagation succeeds within the `RBGC_PROPAGATION_DEADLINE_SEC` budget;
real denial waits the budget and fails cleanly.  This avoids the trap of
treating "denied because forbidden" as "denied because lagging" — the
distinction is observational, not parseable from the 403 response.  The
same time-bound discriminator is already proven in
{rbtgo_access_jwt_probe}'s `invalid_grant` retry harness.

Class C tolerance is **scope-discriminated**: project-scope grant sites
({rbtoe_iam_grant_project}) do not declare 403 tolerance because the
governor's `roles/owner` reaches the project-scope cache effectively
instantly (no observed lag).  Adding spurious 403 tolerance at project
scope would slow down real-denial diagnosis without absorbing any known
race.

lines: proto specifies GET for `getIamPolicy` with no `additional_bindings` for POST. Using POST fails intermittently with HTTP 404 (transcoding layer rejection, not missing repository). See <<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>.

+.. NOTE: Resource-scope IAM cache lag — on a freshly-levied depot, the governor's `roles/owner` grant may not yet have reached the AR-resource-scope IAM cache, even though it has reached project-scope and SA-scope caches.  This surfaces as HTTP 403 on the very first `getIamPolicy` call.  The grant harness absorbs this via class C tolerance (HTTP 403 with empty body-glob, time-bounded against `RBGC_PROPAGATION_DEADLINE_SEC`) — see <<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>> propagation catalog.  Real propagation succeeds within budget; real denial waits the budget and fails cleanly.
HTTP 200 — 404 means repository does not exist (real error, not empty policy)

content.content:
398	## Variable Handling (General Rules)
399	
400	### Expansion Requirements
401	
402	**Always use braced, quoted expansion:**
403	```bash
404	"${z_var}"              # Required pattern - prevents word splitting and glob expansion
405	"${Z_MODULE_VAR}"       # Module variables from kindle
406	"${1:-}"                # Parameters with defaults
407	```
408	
409	**Never use:**
410	```bash
411	$var                    # Unquoted - word splitting risk
412	"$var"                  # Unbraced - boundary ambiguity
413	${var}                  # Unquoted - glob expansion risk
414	```
415	
416	**No tilde in quoted strings** — tilde expansion is suppressed inside quotes:
417	```bash
418	# ❌ Tilde does not expand in quotes
419	"~/Downloads/file.txt"          # Literal ~ character, not home directory
420	
421	# ✅ Use $HOME instead
422	"${HOME}/Downloads/file.txt"    # Expands correctly
423	```
424	
425	**Quote inside parameter expansion operators:**
426	```bash
427	# ✅ Inner expansion quoted separately — matches literally
428	"${z_path%"${z_suffix}"}"       # Strip suffix
429	"${z_path#"${z_prefix}"}"       # Strip prefix
430	
431	# ❌ Inner expansion unquoted — matches as a glob pattern
432	"${z_path%${z_suffix}}"         # z_suffix with * or ? chars matches as glob!
433	"${z_path#${z_prefix}}"         # Same risk
434	```
435	
436	When using `%`, `%%`, `#`, `##` operators with a variable as the pattern, the inner expansion must be quoted separately. Without inner quotes, the pattern is glob-expanded — a suffix containing `*` or `?` silently matches more than intended.
437	
438	### Assignment Patterns
439	
440	```bash
441	# Direct assignment
442	local z_message="Processing item"
443	
444	# ❌ NEVER declare multiple variables on one line
445	local z_name z_value z_result  # Hidden initialization failures!
446	
447	# ✅ ALWAYS one declaration per line with explicit initialization
448	local z_name=""
449	local z_value="default"
450	local z_result=0
451	
452	# ❌ NEVER use local -i — silently coerces non-integer values to 0
453	local -i z_count="${z_input}"  # "abc" becomes 0 — violates no-silent-failures!
454	
455	# ✅ Use plain local with explicit validation
456	local z_count="${z_input}"
457	test "${z_count}" -ge 0 2>/dev/null || buc_die "z_count must be non-negative integer, got: ${z_count}"
458	
459	# ✅ Two-line pattern for captured values (explicit and debuggable)
460	local z_token
461	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
462	
463	# ❌ NEVER combine declaration with capture
464	local z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture)  # Hides exit status!
465	
466	# From file using builtin $(<file) - NOT command substitution, a bash builtin
467	local z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}")
468	test -n "${z_content}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}"
469	
470	# Never split declaration from $(<file) assignment
471	# ❌ local z_content
472	# ❌ z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_FILE}")  # Unnecessary verbosity
473	
474	# Use Capture functions for secrets that must never touch disk
475	local z_token

507	All external command output capture uses temp files. The three prohibited `$()` patterns are: `local z_var=$(cmd)` (local swallows exit status), pipelines inside `$()` (hidden intermediate failures), and unguarded `$()` (silent failure). The only `$()` permitted on functions is for `_capture` (secrets that must not touch disk) and `_recite` (read-only roll access) — both with explicit `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`. 526	# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: Secrets use _capture functions, never temp files
527	# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: _recite functions return via stdout (read-only roll access)

530	**Temp file lifecycle**: Temp files under `BURD_TEMP_DIR` are **preserved after execution** for forensic debugging. Never delete temp files in module code — their persistence is intentional. Cleanup is handled by infrastructure outside BCG's scope.

540

file.content:
## Variable Handling (General Rules)

### Expansion Requirements

**Always use braced, quoted expansion:**
```bash
"${z_var}"              # Required pattern - prevents word splitting and glob expansion
"${Z_MODULE_VAR}"       # Module variables from kindle
"${1:-}"                # Parameters with defaults
```

**Never use:**
```bash
$var                    # Unquoted - word splitting risk
"$var"                  # Unbraced - boundary ambiguity
${var}                  # Unquoted - glob expansion risk
```

**No tilde in quoted strings** — tilde expansion is suppressed inside quotes:
```bash
# ❌ Tilde does not expand in quotes
"~/Downloads/file.txt"          # Literal ~ character, not home directory

# ✅ Use $HOME instead
"${HOME}/Downloads/file.txt"    # Expands correctly
```

**Quote inside parameter expansion operators:**
```bash
# ✅ Inner expansion quoted separately — matches literally
"${z_path%"${z_suffix}"}"       # Strip suffix
"${z_path#"${z_prefix}"}"       # Strip prefix

# ❌ Inner expansion unquoted — matches as a glob pattern
"${z_path%${z_suffix}}"         # z_suffix with * or ? chars matches as glob!
"${z_path#${z_prefix}}"         # Same risk
```

When using `%`, `%%`, `#`, `##` operators with a variable as the pattern, the inner expansion must be quoted separately. Without inner quotes, the pattern is glob-expanded — a suffix containing `*` or `?` silently matches more than intended.

### Assignment Patterns

```bash
# Direct assignment
local z_message="Processing item"

# ❌ NEVER declare multiple variables on one line
local z_name z_value z_result  # Hidden initialization failures!

# ✅ ALWAYS one declaration per line with explicit initialization
local z_name=""
local z_value="default"
local z_result=0

# ❌ NEVER use local -i — silently coerces non-integer values to 0
local -i z_count="${z_input}"  # "abc" becomes 0 — violates no-silent-failures!

# ✅ Use plain local with explicit validation
local z_count="${z_input}"
test "${z_count}" -ge 0 2>/dev/null || buc_die "z_count must be non-negative integer, got: ${z_count}"

# ✅ Two-line pattern for captured values (explicit and debuggable)
local z_token
z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"

# ❌ NEVER combine declaration with capture
local z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture)  # Hides exit status!

# From file using builtin $(<file) - NOT command substitution, a bash builtin
local z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}")
test -n "${z_content}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}"

# Never split declaration from $(<file) assignment
# ❌ local z_content
# ❌ z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_FILE}")  # Unnecessary verbosity

# Use Capture functions for secrets that must never touch disk
local z_token
z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"

# Temp files INSTEAD of command substitution holds intermediate results
echo "${z_result}" > "${Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE}"
complex_command > "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
process < "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP1}" > "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
local z_result=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}")
test -n "${z_result}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}"
```

### Prefer Bash Builtins Over External Tools

```bash
# ✅ String manipulation with parameter expansion
"${z_path##*/}"                    # Instead of: basename
"${z_path%/*}"                     # Instead of: dirname
"${z_string//old/new}"             # Instead of: sed 's/old/new/g'
"${z_string#prefix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/^prefix//'
"${z_string%suffix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/suffix$//'

# ✅ Reading lines with 'read' — guard handles missing trailing newline
while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
  echo "Processing: ${z_line}" || buc_die "Failed to echo line"
done < "${Z_MODULE_INPUT_FILE}"

# ✅ Arithmetic with $((...))
z_result=$((z_value * 2 + 10))    # Instead of: expr or bc
```

### Temp Files Instead of Command Substitution

All external command output capture uses temp files. The three prohibited `$()` patterns are: `local z_var=$(cmd)` (local swallows exit status), pipelines inside `$()` (hidden intermediate failures), and unguarded `$()` (silent failure). The only `$()` permitted on functions is for `_capture` (secrets that must not touch disk) and `_recite` (read-only roll access) — both with explicit `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`. Bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`) is also permitted in `$()` as no file-based alternative exists.

```bash
# ❌ Anti-pattern: nested command substitution
z_result=$(echo $(complex_command) | process)  # Hidden failures!

# ❌ Anti-pattern: unguarded command substitution
z_timestamp=$(date +%s)  # Failure is silent!

# ✅ Correct: temp files make failures visible afterward
complex_command > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
process < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
read -r z_result < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "No result"

# ✅ Correct: single external command via temp file
date +%s > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Failed to get timestamp"
local -r z_timestamp=$(<"${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}")
test -n "${z_timestamp}" || buc_die "Empty timestamp"

# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: Secrets use _capture functions, never temp files
# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: _recite functions return via stdout (read-only roll access)
```

**Temp file lifecycle**: Temp files under `BURD_TEMP_DIR` are **preserved after execution** for forensic debugging. Never delete temp files in module code — their persistence is intentional. Cleanup is handled by infrastructure outside BCG's scope.

### Stderr Capture — Never Suppress

Never use `2>/dev/null` on external commands. Redirect stderr to a temp file so forensic evidence is preserved when commands fail. Include the stderr file path in the `buc_die` message so the user can inspect it.

```bash
# ❌ Anti-pattern: stderr suppressed — failure cause invisible
curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>/dev/null \
  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url}"

# ✅ Correct: stderr captured to temp file, referenced in die message
curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
```

**Stderr file naming**: Use a kindle `_PREFIX` constant (grouped with other temp file prefixes for collision visibility) and append a discriminator at the usage site.

**In loops**, use an auto-incrementing integer for uniqueness rather than trusting loop data values to be unique:

```bash
# In kindle — grouped with sibling temp file prefixes
Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_"

# In command function — integer discriminator ensures uniqueness
local z_index=0
while IFS='|' read -r z_varname z_image z_tag || test -n "${z_varname}"; do
  local z_manifest_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
  local z_stderr_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_inspect_stderr.txt"

  docker manifest inspect "${z_image}:${z_tag}" \
    > "${z_manifest_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to inspect ${z_image}:${z_tag} — see ${z_stderr_file}"

  z_index=$((z_index + 1))
done
```

### String Usage

```bash
# Single-line HERE-string allowed for piping variables
jq '.field' <<<"${z_json_data}"
grep "pattern" <<<"${z_content}"

# Multi-line HERE-strings and HERE-docs prohibited
```

### Readonly Patterns

Readonly enforcement makes mutation bugs loud at the point of violation rather than silent cascades downstream. Three patterns at different scopes:

#### `local -r` — Default for Local Variables

Use `local -r` for every local variable that is assigned once and never modified. This is the majority of locals in a typical BCG function.

```bash
# ✅ Default: readonly locals
local -r z_filepath="${1:-}"
local -r z_varname="${z_buv_varname_roll[$z_idx]}"
local -r z_full_ref="${z_image}@${z_digest}"
local -r z_content=$(<"${z_file}")    # $(<file) is a builtin — safe on one line
```

**Four exceptions** where `local` (mutable) is required:

```bash
# Exception 1: Loop counters

content.content:
594	**Four exceptions** where `local` (mutable) is required:
595	
596	```bash
597	# Exception 1: Loop counters
598	local z_index=0
599	z_index=$((z_index + 1))
600	
601	# Exception 2: Per-iteration synthesized variables (reassigned each pass)
602	local z_manifest_file=""
603	for z_spec in "${z_specs[@]}"; do
604	  z_manifest_file="${Z_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
605	  ...
606	done
607	
608	# Exception 3: Two-line capture pattern (readonly would lock empty value)
609	local z_token
610	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
611	
612	# Exception 4: Accumulators and state-tracking flags
613	local z_any_failed=0
614	local z_current_group=""
615	```
616	
617	**Why not `local -i`?** `local -i` silently coerces non-integers to 0 — it hides bugs. `local -r` does the opposite — it surfaces mutation bugs loudly. Same flag syntax, opposite safety properties.
618	
619	#### `readonly` — Every Kindle Constant
620	
621	Every variable assigned in a kindle function is a constant. Apply `readonly` to every assignment — whether the value is a literal, computed from other variables, or composed from prior assignments:
622	
623	```bash
624	z«prefix»_kindle() {
625	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
626	
627	  # Simple kindle constants (literal values, but defined in kindle for readonly enforcement)
628	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_API_VERSION="v2"
629	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_MAX_RETRIES=3
630	
631	  # Computed from already-set values
632	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_REGISTRY_HOST="${RBGD_LOCATION}${RBGC_HOST_SUFFIX}"
633	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"
634	
635	  # Composed from prior kindle assignments
636	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_ACCEPT_TYPES="${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_A},${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_B}"
637	
638	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
639	}
640	```
641	
642	If a kindle function uses intermediate variables to build up a result (e.g., composing array elements), the final assignment is still `readonly`. Intermediates that are truly local to the build-up should use `local -r`.
643	
644	**The KINDLED sentinel** is a kindle constant — it gets `readonly` like every other kindle assignment. The `test -z` guard at the top of kindle fires before any reassignment attempt, so `readonly` is belt-and-suspenders: the guard gives a friendly error message, `readonly` is the backstop.
645	
646	#### Mutable Kindle State
647	
648	Some kindle functions initialize mutable state alongside constants — counters, accumulators, registry rolls, and builder state that are modified after kindle returns. These variables **must not** be `readonly`, but they must be visually distinguishable from kindle constants.
649	
650	**Convention**: Mutable kindle state uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name`. Constants use **UPPERCASE** `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`. Case alone signals mutability.
651	
652	```bash
653	z«prefix»_kindle() {
654	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
655	
656	  # ✅ Kindle constants — UPPERCASE, readonly
657	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"
658	
659	  # ✅ Mutable kindle state — lowercase, NO readonly
660	  z_«prefix»_file_index=0              # counter incremented post-kindle
661	  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()              # registry populated by enroll calls
662	  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""           # builder state set during enrollment
663	
664	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
665	}
666	```
667	
668	**Rules**:
669	- Mutable kindle state follows the same `z_` internal prefix as roll arrays
670	- Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state
671	- If a variable is initialized in kindle and never mutated afterward, it is a constant — use `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`
672	- The `_roll` suffix is conventional for parallel-array registries but not required for all mutable state (counters, flags, builder state use descriptive names)
673	
674	**Test reset**: When tests need fresh enrollment state, provide an internal reset function that clears mutable kindle state without touching `KINDLED`. Re-kindling a module is never correct — the sentinel is `readonly`.
675	
676	```bash
677	# ✅ Test support: reset mutable state, module stays kindled
678	z«prefix»_reset_enrollment() {
679	  z«prefix»_sentinel
680	  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()
681	  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""
682	  # ... clear all mutable kindle state ...
683	}
684	```
685	
686	**The re-source trap** — `readonly` belongs in code, never in sourceable `.env` files:
687	```bash
688	# ❌ NEVER in sourceable .env files
689	# If a .env file contains `readonly FOO=bar`, sourcing it a second time
690	# (e.g., regime reload, test re-initialization) dies with:
691	#   bash: FOO: readonly variable
692	```
693	
694	#### `readonly VAR` — Lock After Enforce
695	
696	For regime variables loaded from `.env` files, lock them *after* validation succeeds. This is the regime archetype's lock step (see Regime Module Archetype above):
697	
698	```bash
699	z«prefix»_enforce() {
700	  z«prefix»_sentinel
701	  buv_vet «SCOPE»
702	  # ... custom format checks ...
703	}
704	
705	# In the lock step (called after enforce succeeds):
706	# Lock all enrolled variables — any downstream mutation is now a loud error
707	readonly REGI_FIELD_ONE REGI_FIELD_TWO REGI_FIELD_THREE
708	
709	# Derived state built from validated values:
710	Z«PREFIX»_DOCKER_ENV=("-e" "REGI_FIELD_ONE=${REGI_FIELD_ONE}")
711	```
712	
713	**Ordering matters**: Build derived state (docker env arrays, rollup strings) *after* enforce but *before* or *during* the lock call. Once variables are readonly, derived state captures validated, immutable values.
714	
715	## Special Function Definitions
716	
717	### Special Function Patterns
718	
719	This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.
720	
721	| Type      | Pattern                        | First Line           | Returns                      | buc_step                  | Contract                            |
722	|-----------|--------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------------|
723	| Predicate | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_predicate` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | 0=true, 1=false              | No (use buc_log_«source») | Never dies, status only             |

**Four exceptions** where `local` (mutable) is required:

```bash
# Exception 1: Loop counters
local z_index=0
z_index=$((z_index + 1))

# Exception 2: Per-iteration synthesized variables (reassigned each pass)
local z_manifest_file=""
for z_spec in "${z_specs[@]}"; do
  z_manifest_file="${Z_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
  ...
done

# Exception 3: Two-line capture pattern (readonly would lock empty value)
local z_token
z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"

# Exception 4: Accumulators and state-tracking flags
local z_any_failed=0
local z_current_group=""
```

**Why not `local -i`?** `local -i` silently coerces non-integers to 0 — it hides bugs. `local -r` does the opposite — it surfaces mutation bugs loudly. Same flag syntax, opposite safety properties.

#### `readonly` — Every Kindle Constant

Every variable assigned in a kindle function is a constant. Apply `readonly` to every assignment — whether the value is a literal, computed from other variables, or composed from prior assignments:

```bash
z«prefix»_kindle() {
  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"

  # Simple kindle constants (literal values, but defined in kindle for readonly enforcement)
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_API_VERSION="v2"
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_MAX_RETRIES=3

  # Computed from already-set values
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_REGISTRY_HOST="${RBGD_LOCATION}${RBGC_HOST_SUFFIX}"
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"

  # Composed from prior kindle assignments
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_ACCEPT_TYPES="${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_A},${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_B}"

  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
}
```

If a kindle function uses intermediate variables to build up a result (e.g., composing array elements), the final assignment is still `readonly`. Intermediates that are truly local to the build-up should use `local -r`.

**The KINDLED sentinel** is a kindle constant — it gets `readonly` like every other kindle assignment. The `test -z` guard at the top of kindle fires before any reassignment attempt, so `readonly` is belt-and-suspenders: the guard gives a friendly error message, `readonly` is the backstop.

#### Mutable Kindle State

Some kindle functions initialize mutable state alongside constants — counters, accumulators, registry rolls, and builder state that are modified after kindle returns. These variables **must not** be `readonly`, but they must be visually distinguishable from kindle constants.

**Convention**: Mutable kindle state uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name`. Constants use **UPPERCASE** `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`. Case alone signals mutability.

```bash
z«prefix»_kindle() {
  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"

  # ✅ Kindle constants — UPPERCASE, readonly
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"

  # ✅ Mutable kindle state — lowercase, NO readonly
  z_«prefix»_file_index=0              # counter incremented post-kindle
  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()              # registry populated by enroll calls
  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""           # builder state set during enrollment

  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
}
```

**Rules**:
- Mutable kindle state follows the same `z_` internal prefix as roll arrays
- Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state
- If a variable is initialized in kindle and never mutated afterward, it is a constant — use `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`
- The `_roll` suffix is conventional for parallel-array registries but not required for all mutable state (counters, flags, builder state use descriptive names)

**Test reset**: When tests need fresh enrollment state, provide an internal reset function that clears mutable kindle state without touching `KINDLED`. Re-kindling a module is never correct — the sentinel is `readonly`.

```bash
# ✅ Test support: reset mutable state, module stays kindled
z«prefix»_reset_enrollment() {
  z«prefix»_sentinel
  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()
  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""
  # ... clear all mutable kindle state ...
}
```

**The re-source trap** — `readonly` belongs in code, never in sourceable `.env` files:
```bash
# ❌ NEVER in sourceable .env files
# If a .env file contains `readonly FOO=bar`, sourcing it a second time
# (e.g., regime reload, test re-initialization) dies with:
#   bash: FOO: readonly variable
```

#### `readonly VAR` — Lock After Enforce

For regime variables loaded from `.env` files, lock them *after* validation succeeds. This is the regime archetype's lock step (see Regime Module Archetype above):

```bash
z«prefix»_enforce() {
  z«prefix»_sentinel
  buv_vet «SCOPE»
  # ... custom format checks ...
}

# In the lock step (called after enforce succeeds):
# Lock all enrolled variables — any downstream mutation is now a loud error
readonly REGI_FIELD_ONE REGI_FIELD_TWO REGI_FIELD_THREE

# Derived state built from validated values:
Z«PREFIX»_DOCKER_ENV=("-e" "REGI_FIELD_ONE=${REGI_FIELD_ONE}")
```

**Ordering matters**: Build derived state (docker env arrays, rollup strings) *after* enforce but *before* or *during* the lock call. Once variables are readonly, derived state captures validated, immutable values.

## Special Function Definitions

### Special Function Patterns

This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.

| Type      | Pattern                        | First Line           | Returns                      | buc_step                  | Contract                            |
|-----------|--------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Predicate | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_predicate` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | 0=true, 1=false              | No (use buc_log_«source») | Never dies, status only             |

1236	A **commit-message comment** describes the *change event* rather than the *code*. It narrates what was added, when, or why it was inserted — information that belongs in `git log`, not in the file. The tell: the comment would make more sense as a commit summary than as documentation for a future reader.

1240	# Added qualification subsystem for shellcheck support

The resulting comments assume a supervisory reader with conversation context — an audience that does not exist at maintenance time.

1261	## Interface Contamination Discipline
1262	
1263	**Interface contamination** is any change that expands the accepted input space, invocation forms, or alternative paths through an interface without explicit specification requirement.
1264	
1265	### Zeroes Theory
1266	
1267	Every tolerance, alias, fallback, or alternative path multiplies the enumerated state space. The litmus test: **"How many zeroes got added to the enumerated state space because of this choice?"** If the answer isn't zero, the change requires explicit justification.
1268	
1269	### Rules
1270	
1271	1. **One canonical form**: Every command, argument, and parameter has exactly one accepted form. Do not accept alternatives.
1272	2. **No tolerances**: Do not add case folding, prefix stripping, alias acceptance, or alternative parse paths. If the canonical form is `--racing`, do not also accept `-r`, `racing`, or `RACING`.

1278	
1279	If a command can be invoked two different ways for the same result, the interface is contaminated. If `"${1:-}"` silently provides a default for what should be a mandatory argument, the interface is contaminated.

file.content:
### ❌ HERE Documents (Heredocs)

```bash
# Never use - breaks indentation
cat <<EOF > "${z_file}"
content here
must be unindented
EOF

# Use temp files or echo sequences instead
echo "content here" > "${z_file}"
echo "stays indented" >> "${z_file}"
```

### ❌ Bash 4+ Features

```bash
# Avoid these (bash 3.2 compatibility)
declare -A z_array              # Associative arrays
z_files=(**/*.txt)              # ** globbing
readarray -t z_lines            # readarray/mapfile
```

### ❌ Bare File Truncation

```bash
# ❌ Looks like a line where the command was accidentally deleted
> "${z_file}"

# ✅ Explicit no-op makes truncation intent unambiguous
: > "${z_file}"
```

Bare `> file` without a command is valid bash but reads like an accident at 3am. The `: >` form explicitly signals "I am creating or truncating this file." Shellcheck enforces this via SC2188.

**Prefer immutable temp files.** BCG temp files are forensic artifacts — preserved for post-mortem debugging. If you find yourself truncating a temp file, the design likely needs a unique filename per step instead of reusing and clearing. Truncation is appropriate only for log files and other non-forensic outputs.

### ❌ Inline Shellcheck Directives

```bash
# ❌ Never suppress shellcheck inline
# shellcheck disable=SC2086
some_command ${z_unquoted}

# ❌ Never use source path hints
# shellcheck source=/dev/null
source "${z_file}"
```

All BCG-structural shellcheck suppressions live in `busc_shellcheckrc`. No inline `# shellcheck` directives of any kind. If a shellcheck code fires and it's BCG-structural, add it to `busc_shellcheckrc` with rationale. If it's a genuine finding, fix the code.

### ❌ Commit-Message Comments

A **commit-message comment** describes the *change event* rather than the *code*. It narrates what was added, when, or why it was inserted — information that belongs in `git log`, not in the file. The tell: the comment would make more sense as a commit summary than as documentation for a future reader.

```bash
# ❌ Commit-message comment: narrates the insertion
# Added qualification subsystem for shellcheck support
buw-qsc) exec "${z_buq_cli}" buq_shellcheck "$@" ;;

# ❌ Commit-message comment: documents the change, not the code
# New helper for retry logic (see PR #142)
zrbf_retry_with_backoff() {

# ✅ No comment needed — the code is self-evident
buw-qsc) exec "${z_buq_cli}" buq_shellcheck "$@" ;;

# ✅ Comment describes the code, not the change
# Retry with exponential backoff; max delay capped at 60s
zrbf_retry_with_backoff() {
```

**Litmus test**: Would this comment be better as a commit message? If yes, delete it from the code — it's already captured in git history.

This is a characteristic LLM failure mode. Models narrate their own work process into code, writing for the person reviewing the diff rather than the person reading the file next year. The resulting comments assume a supervisory reader with conversation context — an audience that does not exist at maintenance time.

---

## Interface Contamination Discipline

**Interface contamination** is any change that expands the accepted input space, invocation forms, or alternative paths through an interface without explicit specification requirement.

### Zeroes Theory

Every tolerance, alias, fallback, or alternative path multiplies the enumerated state space. The litmus test: **"How many zeroes got added to the enumerated state space because of this choice?"** If the answer isn't zero, the change requires explicit justification.

### Rules

1. **One canonical form**: Every command, argument, and parameter has exactly one accepted form. Do not accept alternatives.
2. **No tolerances**: Do not add case folding, prefix stripping, alias acceptance, or alternative parse paths. If the canonical form is `--racing`, do not also accept `-r`, `racing`, or `RACING`.
3. **No undocumented defaults**: Do not add `${1:-default}` patterns that silently fill missing required arguments. Required means required.
4. **No silent normalization**: Do not silently transform input. If input doesn't match the canonical form, `buc_die` with a clear message stating the expected form.
5. **Documentation uses canonical form**: All documentation, examples, and `buc_doc_*` output must show the single canonical invocation — never a tolerated alternative.

### Smell Test

If a command can be invoked two different ways for the same result, the interface is contaminated. If `"${1:-}"` silently provides a default for what should be a mandatory argument, the interface is contaminated.

---

1	# busc_shellcheckrc — BCG-structural shellcheck suppressions
2	#
3	# These codes are false positives caused by BCG's module architecture:
4	# dynamic sourcing via BURD_* paths, cross-module kindle/literal constants,
5	# and indirect function invocation via source/dispatch.
6	#
7	# Policy: No inline shellcheck directives anywhere in the codebase.
8	# All structural suppressions live here. Genuine findings must be fixed.
9	#
10	# Companion: BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md § Shellcheck Integration
11	
12	# SC1090: Can't follow non-constant source.
13	#   BCG furnish pattern sources via "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/module.sh" —
14	#   shellcheck cannot resolve variable-based paths.
15	disable=SC1090
16	
17	# SC1091: Not following: file was not specified as input.
18	#   Same root cause as SC1090. Shellcheck doesn't know sourced files
19	#   exist because paths are computed at runtime.
20	disable=SC1091
21	
22	# SC2034: Variable appears unused.
23	#   Literal constants and kindle constants are defined in one module
24	#   and consumed by other modules after sourcing. Shellcheck sees
25	#   assignment but not the cross-module usage.
26	disable=SC2034
27	
28	# SC2154: Variable referenced but not assigned.
29	#   Inverse of SC2034. Variables defined in sourced modules (kindle
30	#   constants, literal constants, regime fields) are invisible to
31	#   shellcheck in the consuming file.
32	disable=SC2154
33	
34	# SC2155: Declare and assign separately to avoid masking return values.
35	#   BCG explicitly blesses single-line $(<file) assignment:
36	#     local z_content=$(<"${Z_FILE}")
37	#   This is a bash builtin, not command substitution. BCG requires the
38	#   two-line pattern only for _capture functions using $().
39	disable=SC2155
40	
41	# SC2153: Possible misspelling.
42	#   Cross-module variable names with similar prefixes (e.g., ZRBGU_PREFIX
43	#   used in rbgb module) trigger false misspelling suggestions.
44	disable=SC2153
45	
46	# SC2329: Function is never invoked.
47	#   BUK functions are invoked indirectly via source and dispatch.
48	#   Shellcheck cannot trace the dynamic call graph.
49	disable=SC2329
50	
51	# SC2016: Expressions don't expand in single quotes.
52	#   BCG template-generation patterns (buut_tabtarget.sh, buq_qualify.sh,
53	#   cloud build scripts) echo single-quoted strings containing ${} into
54	#   generated files (tabtargets, launchers, Dockerfiles). The expressions
55	#   are intended for the generated file's runtime, not the generator's.
56	disable=SC2016
57	
58	# SC2254: Quote expansions in case patterns to match literally.
59	#   BCG glob-matching patterns intentionally use unquoted variables in
60	#   case statements (e.g., exemption patterns in buq_qualify.sh).
61	#   The glob expansion is the desired behavior.
62	disable=SC2254
63	
64	# SC2059: Don't use variables in the printf format string.
65	#   BCG table-formatting patterns define a local -r format constant
66	#   (e.g., z_row_fmt) used across header, separator, and data rows.
67	#   The variable is always a locally-defined constant, never user input.
68	disable=SC2059
69

file.content:
# busc_shellcheckrc — BCG-structural shellcheck suppressions
#
# These codes are false positives caused by BCG's module architecture:
# dynamic sourcing via BURD_* paths, cross-module kindle/literal constants,
# and indirect function invocation via source/dispatch.
#
# Policy: No inline shellcheck directives anywhere in the codebase.
# All structural suppressions live here. Genuine findings must be fixed.
#
# Companion: BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md § Shellcheck Integration

# SC1090: Can't follow non-constant source.
#   BCG furnish pattern sources via "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/module.sh" —
#   shellcheck cannot resolve variable-based paths.
disable=SC1090

# SC1091: Not following: file was not specified as input.
#   Same root cause as SC1090. Shellcheck doesn't know sourced files
#   exist because paths are computed at runtime.
disable=SC1091

# SC2034: Variable appears unused.
#   Literal constants and kindle constants are defined in one module
#   and consumed by other modules after sourcing. Shellcheck sees
#   assignment but not the cross-module usage.
disable=SC2034

# SC2154: Variable referenced but not assigned.
#   Inverse of SC2034. Variables defined in sourced modules (kindle
#   constants, literal constants, regime fields) are invisible to
#   shellcheck in the consuming file.
disable=SC2154

# SC2155: Declare and assign separately to avoid masking return values.
#   BCG explicitly blesses single-line $(<file) assignment:
#     local z_content=$(<"${Z_FILE}")
#   This is a bash builtin, not command substitution. BCG requires the
#   two-line pattern only for _capture functions using $().
disable=SC2155

# SC2153: Possible misspelling.
#   Cross-module variable names with similar prefixes (e.g., ZRBGU_PREFIX
#   used in rbgb module) trigger false misspelling suggestions.
disable=SC2153

# SC2329: Function is never invoked.
#   BUK functions are invoked indirectly via source and dispatch.
#   Shellcheck cannot trace the dynamic call graph.
disable=SC2329

# SC2016: Expressions don't expand in single quotes.
#   BCG template-generation patterns (buut_tabtarget.sh, buq_qualify.sh,
#   cloud build scripts) echo single-quoted strings containing ${} into
#   generated files (tabtargets, launchers, Dockerfiles). The expressions
#   are intended for the generated file's runtime, not the generator's.
disable=SC2016

# SC2254: Quote expansions in case patterns to match literally.
#   BCG glob-matching patterns intentionally use unquoted variables in
#   case statements (e.g., exemption patterns in buq_qualify.sh).
#   The glob expansion is the desired behavior.
disable=SC2254

# SC2059: Don't use variables in the printf format string.
#   BCG table-formatting patterns define a local -r format constant
#   (e.g., z_row_fmt) used across header, separator, and data rows.
#   The variable is always a locally-defined constant, never user input.
disable=SC2059

input.params: {"identity": "BBABF", "files": ["Tools/rbk/rbgc_Constants.sh", "Tools/rbk/rbgi_IAM.sh", "Tools/rbk/rbgg_Governor.sh", "Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSCIP-IamPropagation.adoc", "Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSCIG-IamGrantContracts.adoc", "Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDK-director_invest.adoc", "Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSGM-governor_mantle.adoc", "Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDE-depot_levy.adoc", "Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRK-retriever_invest.adoc"], "intent": "Close class C propagation gap surfaced by ₣A_'s fresh-depot run on canest2bhm100003 (memo-20260513-iam-propagation-race-director-invest-gar): rbgg_invest_director's AR getIamPolicy failed HTTP 403 against a freshly-empowered governor (~90s after roles/owner grant), where the same script's earlier project-scope and SA-scope IAM operations succeeded against the same token. Generalize zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate to take per-site (code, body-glob) tolerance pairs (empty glob = match-any-body, short-circuits without loading the error message), mint RBGC_PROPAGATION_{INITIAL_DELAY,MAX_DELAY,DEADLINE}_SEC=3/20/420 as the single shared budget for every IAM-grant retry site, and add (403, \"\") tolerance to the four resource-scope rbgi_* functions (repo, SA, bucket, secret) plus the inline GAR retry in rbgg_invest_director. Project-scope rbgi_add_project_iam_role keeps only the two 400 member-visibility pairs — the governor's roles/owner grant reaches project-scope IAM caches effectively instantly, so spurious 403 tolerance there would slow real-denial diagnosis without absorbing any known race. Class C uses time-bound discrimination rather than body fingerprinting (proven model from rbgv_jwt_sa_probe): the bound IS the discriminator — real propagation succeeds within RBGC_PROPAGATION_DEADLINE_SEC, real denial waits the budget and fails cleanly. Tier A spec sync: RBSCIP gains a three-class taxonomy at the top (A forward member-visibility / B backward member-visibility / C caller-recently-empowered) and replaces inline 3/20/420 backoff numbers in the Backoff Profile section with RBGC_PROPAGATION_* constant references plus a load-bearing note that class C is time-bounded only and that the shared budget propagates by editing the constants once; RBSCIG gains a 'Propagation Error Catalog' table covering all three classes (code / body glob / scope applicability / discriminator) followed by prose explaining class C's empirical evidence (2026-05-13 fresh-depot AR repo 403 against governor with ~30s-old roles/owner grant, cumulative additional propagation ~30s) and the project-vs-resource scope discriminator; RBSDK's AR step gains a NOTE about resource-scope cache lag and class C absorption with reference to RBSCIG. Tier B coherence: RBSGM notes that downstream resource-scope ops absorb the roles/owner propagation lag via class C tolerance; RBSDE notes that the existing artifactregistry-API preflight poll does NOT gate per-repository resource-scope cache state (in-band absorption in director-invest does); RBSRK notes that its project-scope grant doesn't need class C tolerance, distinguishing it from director-invest even though both run shortly after governor mantle. Departures from docket: loop-skeleton extraction deferred per docket (bash higher-order is ugly); Tier C drift (RBS0 mapping, RBSHR closure, RBSAJ cross-refs) deferred to BBAAq's running-list mechanism. BCG audit pass on touched code: five `local z_prop_deadline=` upgraded to `local -r` (assigned-once locals get readonly per BCG); inline `# shellcheck disable=SC2254` removed (SC2254 already globally suppressed in busc_shellcheckrc:62); `local -ra z_tolerance=(...)` arrays declared readonly; comments WHY-only (tolerance composition rationale, scope distinction). Verification: bash -n clean on rbgc_Constants.sh / rbgi_IAM.sh / rbgg_Governor.sh; shellcheck delta zero; fast suite 98/98 (enrollment-validation 47, regime-validation 27, regime-smoke 9, handbook-render 15) — handbook-render exercises asciidoctor rendering of all six touched specs."}

content.text:
You're right — that was discipline drift. Two tabtargets I should have used:

- `tt/buw-qsc.QualifyShellCheck.sh` — codebase-wide shellcheck via the kit's qualify path
- `tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh` — fast qualify: tabtargets, colophons, nameplate health (orthogonal from the test suite)

The bare `shellcheck` invocation just lit up those three files against the rcfile — it skipped the rest of the codebase, and it didn't touch tabtarget/colophon/nameplate qualification at all. The fast test suite I ran covers fixture-level qualification but not the codebase-shape checks.

Want me to run `tt/buw-qsc.QualifyShellCheck.sh` and `tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh` now? The pace is wrapped but the post-hoc check is still informative — if anything red turns up, it becomes a follow-up itch or spook capture rather than reopening ₢BBABF.

60:  for z_file in "${z_tt_dir}"/*.sh; do
168:buq_shellcheck() {
170:  local z_rcfile="${2:-${BURD_BUK_DIR:+${BURD_BUK_DIR}/busc_shellcheckrc}}"
171:  local z_result_file="${3:-${BURD_TEMP_DIR:+${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/buq_shellcheck_results.txt}}"
172:  test -n "${z_tools_dir}"   || buc_die "buq_shellcheck: tools directory required"
173:  test -n "${z_rcfile}"      || buc_die "buq_shellcheck: rcfile path required"
174:  test -n "${z_result_file}" || buc_die "buq_shellcheck: result file path required"
176:  buc_step "Running shellcheck qualification"
181:  command -v shellcheck >/dev/null 2>&1 || buc_die "shellcheck not found — install from https://www.shellcheck.net"
183:  # Collect .sh files (load-then-iterate per BCG)
188:  done < <(find "${z_tools_dir}" -name '*.sh' -type f | sort)
193:  test "${z_file_count}" -gt 0 || buc_die "No .sh files found under ${z_tools_dir}"
195:  # Run shellcheck — capture output to temp file for forensics
197:  shellcheck --rcfile="${z_rcfile}" -S style -f gcc "${z_files[@]}" \
206:  # Count and display findings
207:  local z_finding_count=0
210:    z_finding_count=$((z_finding_count + 1))
212:  buc_log_args "Shellcheck findings: ${z_finding_count} (see ${z_result_file})"
219:  buc_die "Shellcheck qualification failed: ${z_finding_count} findings across ${z_file_count} files"

165	######################################################################
166	# Shellcheck qualification
167	
168	buq_shellcheck() {
169	  local z_tools_dir="${1:-${BURD_TOOLS_DIR:-}}"
170	  local z_rcfile="${2:-${BURD_BUK_DIR:+${BURD_BUK_DIR}/busc_shellcheckrc}}"
171	  local z_result_file="${3:-${BURD_TEMP_DIR:+${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/buq_shellcheck_results.txt}}"
172	  test -n "${z_tools_dir}"   || buc_die "buq_shellcheck: tools directory required"
173	  test -n "${z_rcfile}"      || buc_die "buq_shellcheck: rcfile path required"
174	  test -n "${z_result_file}" || buc_die "buq_shellcheck: result file path required"
175	
176	  buc_step "Running shellcheck qualification"
177	
178	  test -f "${z_rcfile}"    || buc_die "Shellcheck rcfile not found: ${z_rcfile}"
179	  test -d "${z_tools_dir}" || buc_die "Tools directory not found: ${z_tools_dir}"
180	
181	  command -v shellcheck >/dev/null 2>&1 || buc_die "shellcheck not found — install from https://www.shellcheck.net"
182	
183	  # Collect .sh files (load-then-iterate per BCG)
184	  local z_files=()
185	  local z_file=""
186	  while IFS= read -r z_file || test -n "${z_file}"; do
187	    z_files+=("${z_file}")
188	  done < <(find "${z_tools_dir}" -name '*.sh' -type f | sort)
189	
190	  local -r z_file_count=${#z_files[@]}
191	  buc_log_args "Found ${z_file_count} shell files under ${z_tools_dir}"
192	
193	  test "${z_file_count}" -gt 0 || buc_die "No .sh files found under ${z_tools_dir}"
194	
195	  # Run shellcheck — capture output to temp file for forensics
196	  local z_status=0
197	  shellcheck --rcfile="${z_rcfile}" -S style -f gcc "${z_files[@]}" \
198	    > "${z_result_file}" 2>&1 \
199	    || z_status=$?
200	
201	  if test "${z_status}" = "0"; then
202	    buc_step "Shellcheck qualification passed: ${z_file_count} files clean"
203	    return 0
204	  fi
205	
206	  # Count and display findings
207	  local z_finding_count=0
208	  local z_discard
209	  while IFS= read -r z_discard || test -n "${z_discard:-}"; do
210	    z_finding_count=$((z_finding_count + 1))
211	  done < "${z_result_file}"
212	  buc_log_args "Shellcheck findings: ${z_finding_count} (see ${z_result_file})"
213	
214	  local z_line=""
215	  while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
216	    buc_warn "${z_line}"
217	  done < "${z_result_file}"
218	
219	  buc_die "Shellcheck qualification failed: ${z_finding_count} findings across ${z_file_count} files"
220	}
221	
222	# eof

######################################################################
# Shellcheck qualification

buq_shellcheck() {
  local z_tools_dir="${1:-${BURD_TOOLS_DIR:-}}"
  local z_rcfile="${2:-${BURD_BUK_DIR:+${BURD_BUK_DIR}/busc_shellcheckrc}}"
  local z_result_file="${3:-${BURD_TEMP_DIR:+${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/buq_shellcheck_results.txt}}"
  test -n "${z_tools_dir}"   || buc_die "buq_shellcheck: tools directory required"
  test -n "${z_rcfile}"      || buc_die "buq_shellcheck: rcfile path required"
  test -n "${z_result_file}" || buc_die "buq_shellcheck: result file path required"

  buc_step "Running shellcheck qualification"

  test -f "${z_rcfile}"    || buc_die "Shellcheck rcfile not found: ${z_rcfile}"
  test -d "${z_tools_dir}" || buc_die "Tools directory not found: ${z_tools_dir}"

  command -v shellcheck >/dev/null 2>&1 || buc_die "shellcheck not found — install from https://www.shellcheck.net"

  # Collect .sh files (load-then-iterate per BCG)
  local z_files=()
  local z_file=""
  while IFS= read -r z_file || test -n "${z_file}"; do
    z_files+=("${z_file}")
  done < <(find "${z_tools_dir}" -name '*.sh' -type f | sort)

  local -r z_file_count=${#z_files[@]}
  buc_log_args "Found ${z_file_count} shell files under ${z_tools_dir}"

  test "${z_file_count}" -gt 0 || buc_die "No .sh files found under ${z_tools_dir}"

  # Run shellcheck — capture output to temp file for forensics
  local z_status=0
  shellcheck --rcfile="${z_rcfile}" -S style -f gcc "${z_files[@]}" \
    > "${z_result_file}" 2>&1 \
    || z_status=$?

  if test "${z_status}" = "0"; then
    buc_step "Shellcheck qualification passed: ${z_file_count} files clean"
    return 0
  fi

  # Count and display findings
  local z_finding_count=0
  local z_discard
  while IFS= read -r z_discard || test -n "${z_discard:-}"; do
    z_finding_count=$((z_finding_count + 1))
  done < "${z_result_file}"
  buc_log_args "Shellcheck findings: ${z_finding_count} (see ${z_result_file})"

  local z_line=""
  while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
    buc_warn "${z_line}"
  done < "${z_result_file}"

  buc_die "Shellcheck qualification failed: ${z_finding_count} findings across ${z_file_count} files"
}

# eof

buq_tabtargets() {
  local z_tt_dir="${1:-}"
  local z_project_root="${2:-}"
  test -n "${z_tt_dir}"       || buc_die "buq_tabtargets: tabtarget directory required"
  test -n "${z_project_root}" || buc_die "buq_tabtargets: project root required"
  test -d "${z_tt_dir}"       || buc_die "buq_tabtargets: directory not found: ${z_tt_dir}"
  shift 2

  # Remaining arguments are glob patterns for exempt tabtargets
  local z_exemptions=("$@")

  buc_step "Qualifying tabtarget structure in ${z_tt_dir}"

  local z_fail_files=()
  local z_fail_reasons=()
  local z_count=0
  local z_exempt_count=0

  # Prescribed tabtarget form (from buut_tabtarget.sh generator):
  #   Line 1:      #!/bin/bash
  #   Line 2:      export BURD_LAUNCHER="<launcher_path>"
  #   Lines 3..N-1: optional export BURD_*=* flag lines
  #   Last line:   exec "${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}/../${BURD_LAUNCHER}" "${0##*/}" "${@}"
  local z_prescribed_shebang='#!/bin/bash'
  local z_prescribed_exec='exec "${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}/../${BURD_LAUNCHER}" "${0##*/}" "${@}"'

  local z_file=""
  for z_file in "${z_tt_dir}"/*.sh; do
    test -e "${z_file}" || continue
    z_count=$((z_count + 1))

    local z_basename="${z_file##*/}"

    # Check exemption patterns
    local z_is_exempt=0
    local z_exempt_pattern=""
    for z_exempt_pattern in "${z_exemptions[@]+"${z_exemptions[@]}"}"; do
      case "${z_basename}" in
        ${z_exempt_pattern}) z_is_exempt=1; break ;;
      esac
    done
    if test "${z_is_exempt}" = "1"; then
      z_exempt_count=$((z_exempt_count + 1))
      continue
    fi

    # Load file lines (load-then-iterate per BCG)
    local z_lines=()
    local z_line=""
    while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
      z_lines+=("${z_line}")
    done < "${z_file}"

    local z_num_lines=${#z_lines[@]}

    # Must have at least 3 lines: shebang, BURD_LAUNCHER, exec
    test "${z_num_lines}" -ge 3 || {
      z_fail_files+=("${z_basename}")
      z_fail_reasons+=("too few lines: ${z_num_lines} (minimum 3)")
      continue
    }

    # Line 1: prescribed shebang
    test "${z_lines[0]}" = "${z_prescribed_shebang}" || {
      z_fail_files+=("${z_basename}")
      z_fail_reasons+=("line 1: expected '${z_prescribed_shebang}', got '${z_lines[0]}'")
      continue
    }

    # Line 2: must be export BURD_LAUNCHER="..."

content.text:
Coverage map across the two tabtargets:

**`buw-qsc.QualifyShellCheck.sh`** (and `rbw-tr` Phase 2)
- `find "${BURC_TOOLS_DIR}" -name '*.sh' -type f` → 176 files
- `shellcheck --rcfile=busc_shellcheckrc -S style -f gcc` — most aggressive severity, structural suppressions only
- Pre-existing 30 findings, zero in files I touched

**`rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh`** (`rbq_qualify_fast`)
- `buq_tabtargets` — structural shape check on every `tt/*.sh` (line 1 shebang, line 2 BURD_LAUNCHER, last line prescribed exec) for all 207 tabtargets
- `rbq_qualify_colophons` — colophon registration
- `rbq_qualify_context` — generated tabtarget-context freshness vs. zipper
- `rbrn_preflight` — nameplate preflight

**Genuine coverage gaps** (files outside `Tools/` that look like real bash):

| Path | Lines | Risk | Notes |
|------|-------|------|-------|
| `.buk/launcher.*.sh` × 13 | 3–4 each | ✱ very low | Trivial dispatch shims |
| `.buk/rbbc_constants.sh` | 34 | moderate | Real content, **fully uncovered** |
| `tt/*.sh` × 207 | 3 each | ✱ very low | Generated; shape-qualified instead |
| `MBS.STATION-reference.sh` (root) | ? | unknown | Worth a peek |
| `Study/study_workbench.sh` | ? | unknown | Possibly intentional (exploratory area) |

The `tt/`-files-not-in-shellcheck gap is by design (shape qualification is the appropriate gate for generated shims), but `.buk/rbbc_constants.sh` and the root-level `MBS.STATION-reference.sh` look like real-content blind spots.

For my own touched code, every file lives under `Tools/rbk/` — all in scope. The direct shellcheck I ran matched what the kit would have flagged: zero new findings against the baseline 30.

Want me to capture the `.buk/rbbc_constants.sh` gap as a spook/itch in ₣BB, or is this expected scope?

content.text:
Proposed docket draft for review before enrolling:

**Silks:** `fast-qualify-shellcheck-fold-cover-zero`

```
## Character

Mechanical. Fold the two fast-codebase-check entry points into one tier, parallelize the slow one, expand its coverage, and bring its baseline to zero.

## Locked decisions

- **One fast-tier shellcheck entry.** `rbq_qualify_fast` absorbs `buq_shellcheck`; standalone `tt/buw-qsc.QualifyShellCheck.sh` retires. The primitive function stays; only the standalone tabtarget retires. `rbw-tr` drops its now-redundant shellcheck phase.
- **Parallelize via xargs -P.** Replace the single shellcheck invocation with xargs-driven parallel invocation; aggregate exit code into one verdict. Target ~1–2s wall-clock for the file set (down from ~9s).
- **Coverage expansion.** `.buk/rbbc_constants.sh` joins the shellcheck file set. Mount-time call on scope-generalization shape (multi-root parameter vs explicit outside-Tools list).
- **Baseline zero.** Every current shellcheck finding either fixed in-place or moved to `busc_shellcheckrc` with BCG-structural rationale. Fast-tier exits zero on clean tree after wrap.

## Wrap criterion

`tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh` exits zero, runs in ~2s wall-clock, covers `Tools/` plus `.buk/rbbc_constants.sh`. `tt/buw-qsc.QualifyShellCheck.sh` no longer exists. `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` no longer makes a separate shellcheck call.

## Out of scope

- `tt/*.sh` shellcheck coverage (`buq_tabtargets` shape-check is the appropriate gate for generated shims)
- `.buk/launcher.*.sh` (3–4 line trivial shims; capture as itch if ever produces a finding)
- `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/FUTURE/` subtree — operator call at mount (exempt vs fix)
```

Two questions before I write to `gazette_in.md`:

options.description: Fix findings first against current scope. Then fold/parallelize/cover as a clean follow-up. Lets each pace wrap green on its own terms.

options.description: If you read 'fast-tier qualify shape' as testing-infrastructure simplification rather than release qualification.

options.label: ₣BA (rbk-11-mvp-parallel-cleanups)
options.description: If you read baseline cleanup + tabtarget consolidation as a parallel-cleanup itch rather than tier infrastructure work.

questions.question: Pace size — single pace or split?

options.label: Single pace as drafted
options.description: Four threads (fold, parallelize, cover, baseline) land together. Wrap criterion ties them. Bigger pace but coherent.

options.label: Split: infrastructure pace + baseline pace
options.description: First pace lands fold/parallelize/cover with a non-zero-tolerant fast-tier. Second pace fixes the 30 findings. Smaller paces, but the infrastructure pace can't wrap-green-tally until baseline lands.

options.label: Split: baseline first, then infrastructure
options.description: Fix findings first against current scope. Then fold/parallelize/cover as a clean follow-up. Lets each pace wrap green on its own terms.
